pattern: The trailing-zero count of a factorial only ever jumps in steps of five, so the preimage is either empty or contains exactly five consecutive integers; a binary search on the count locates them.
hints: The number of trailing zeroes in x! is determined by how many factors of five appear in 1..x, since fives are always rarer than twos.
That trailing-zero function is non-decreasing in x, so you can binary search for the smallest x whose factorial has at least k zeroes.

Because the count rises by one only at multiples of higher powers of five, every value it hits is hit by exactly five consecutive x. So the answer is always 0 or 5: return 5 if some x achieves exactly k zeroes, otherwise 0.

edge_inputs.description: smallest k, the five factorials 0! through 4! have no trailing zeroes
a count that is skipped entirely, so no x produces it
a reachable count yields exactly five integers
another skipped value (the jump at 25 lands the count above this)
the count just before a double jump is reachable
value right after a power-of-five jump that is unreachable

stress.description: sweep a wide range of k values, mixing reachable and skipped counts
stress.note: random k stresses the binary-search boundary against the brute oracle
